question.text: For Foreign Portfolio Investors (FPIs), gains or losses from exchange-traded derivatives are taxable under:

answer.text: Capital Gains, with most F&O trades generating short-term capital gains/losses due to contract maturities up to 3 months
Profits and Gains from Business or Profession, same as domestic taxpayers
FPIs are exempt from tax on derivative transactions
A flat withholding tax rate of 10% on all derivative gains

explanation.text: **The Logic:** FPIs are treated differently from domestic taxpayers. Their derivative gains/losses are taxable under 'Capital Gains' head (not business income). Since most F&O contracts have maturities up to 3 months, gains are generally short-term capital gains. **The Trap:** Domestic traders = business income. FPIs = capital gains. This is a critical distinction. Don't apply the same tax treatment to both. **Cross-Exam Context:** FPI taxation of derivatives is a specialized topic in Series VIII. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** FPI F&O gains = capital gains (usually short-term). Domestic F&O gains = non-speculative business income.
